title: The Curse of Sparsity
concept: As vocabulary grows, one-hot vectors become massive and mostly empty, leading to memory bloat and slow computations in neural nets.

exercises.prompt: What does a one-hot encoded vector look like?

options.text: Only one number is 1, and the rest are 0
Every number is a random decimal

exercises.explain: One-hot encoding represents each word as a vector where only one element is 1 and the rest are 0.

cards.title: A Mountain of Zeros
cards.body: One-hot encoding seems great at first, but it has a dark secret. As your AI learns more words, its memory usage explodes, and it gets buried in a mountain of zeros! Let's discover why this happens.

cards.title: What is Sparsity?
cards.body: In AI, sparsity means having vectors filled almost entirely with zeros. As your vocabulary grows to thousands of words, each word's one-hot representation becomes a giant list of zeros with just a single '1'. This waste of space is called the Curse of Sparsity.

cards.title: The Empty Box Delivery
cards.body: Imagine a giant dictionary where every single word has its own massive storage box. If you only want to write the word 'cat', you still have to carry around thousands of empty boxes just because those other words exist. One-hot encoding is like carrying all those empty boxes!

cards.title: The 50,000-Word Problem
cards.body: If an AI knows 50,000 words, representing the word 'apple' requires a list of 50,000 numbers. 49,999 of those numbers are zeros! If you process a whole book this way, your computer will quickly run out of memory trying to store all those zeros.

cards.title: Zeros Aren't Free!
cards.body: It's easy to think that adding more zeros is harmless because zero means 'nothing'. But in computers, storing and calculating math with millions of zeros takes up real memory space and slows down processor speeds dramatically!

cards.title: Scaling the Zeros
cards.body: Compare how a small vocabulary looks next to a large vocabulary using one-hot encoding. Notice how the space required explodes!

exercises.prompt: What happens to one-hot vectors as a dictionary grows from 100 words to 100,000 words?

options.text: They grow much longer and contain more zeros
They become filled with random fractions

exercises.explain: As the vocabulary size increases, the length of each one-hot vector grows matching that size, making the vectors mostly empty.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Sparsity makes neural networks run faster and use less memory.
exercises.explain: Sparsity means the vectors are mostly empty (zeros), which wastes memory and degrades computational efficiency.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe why sparse vectors waste space.
exercises.sentence: One-hot vectors become mostly empty because they are filled with thousands of ▢.
exercises.explain: One-hot vectors are mostly empty because only one position represents the active word, leaving all other spots as zero.

exercises.prompt: Which AI model accurately describes the performance impact of a large vocabulary on one-hot encoding?

options.text: AI Model A: 'A larger vocabulary makes one-hot vectors run much faster because they have more zeros to skip.'
AI Model B: 'A larger vocabulary makes one-hot vectors massive, slowing down calculations and wasting computer memory.'
AI Model C: 'One-hot vectors stay the exact same size no matter how many new words you add.'

exercises.explain: AI Model B is correct because one-hot vectors get larger and harder to compute with as the vocabulary size increases.

exercises.prompt: Why is the Curse of Sparsity such a major problem for AI models working with large vocabularies?

options.text: They make models learn too fast
They waste memory and slow down computations
They only work for image data
They cause text to become automatically deleted

exercises.explain: Sparse, giant vectors require massive computer memory and slow down mathematical operations, creating a huge bottleneck for AI models.
